title: (No AI) Google makes it easy to deepfake yourself
author: Robert Hart
content:
![](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/04/acastro_STK092_03.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

YouTube Shorts is [rolling out](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/16985237#zippy=%2Cwill-videos-generated-using-an-avatar-contain-ai-disclosures%2Chow-do-i-submit-feedback-about-avatars-on-youtube%2Chow-is-my-data-collected-used%2Climit-or-remove-remixes-of-videos-using-your-avatar%2Cretake-or-delete-your-avatar-from-the-youtube-create-app%2Cretake-or-delete-your-avatar-from-the-youtube-app) a new AI-powered feature giving creators an easy way to realistically clone themselves on camera. The launch, [hinted at earlier this year](https://www.theverge.com/news/864610/youtube-shorts-ai-likenesses-neal-mohan-2026), reflects the platform's fraught relationship with AI-generated content, adding more generative features while struggling to [contain AI slop](https://www.theverge.com/news/869684/youtube-top-ai-channels-removed-kapwing), deepfake scams, and impersonations.

YouTube says the new tool will let users create a digital version of themselves, called an avatar, that can be inserted into existing Shorts videos or used to generate entirely new ones. The company said avatars will "look and sound like you," framing them as a safer and more secure way to use AI to …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/909104/youtube-shorts-make-ai-avatar)
contentSnippet:
YouTube Shorts is rolling out a new AI-powered feature giving creators an easy way to realistically clone themselves on camera. The launch, hinted at earlier this year, reflects the platform's fraught relationship with AI-generated content, adding more generative features while struggling to contain AI slop, deepfake scams, and impersonations. 
YouTube says the new tool will let users create a digital version of themselves, called an avatar, that can be inserted into existing Shorts videos or used to generate entirely new ones. The company said avatars will "look and sound like you," framing them as a safer and more secure way to use AI to  …
Read the full story at The Verge.

The launch, hinted at earlier this year, reflects the platform's fraught relationship with AI-generated content, adding more generative features while struggling to contain AI slop, deepfake scams, and impersonations. title: (No AI) Gemini gets notebooks to help you organize projects
author: Jay Peters
content:
![Illustration of the Google Gemini logo.](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2025/02/STK255_Google_Gemini_A.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

Google's Gemini is getting a feature called "notebooks" to help you organize things about certain topics in a single place while using the AI chatbot, [the company announced on Wednesday](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/products/gemini-app/notebooks-gemini-notebooklm/). You can pull in things like files, past conversations, and custom instructions into notebooks that Gemini can then use as context while you're talking with it.

Notebooks sound a lot like ChatGPT's Projects feature, which launched [in 2024](https://www.theverge.com/2024/12/13/24320800/openai-chatgpt-projects-folders-ai-chats) and similarly [lets users](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/10169521-projects-in-chatgpt) store things about a certain topic in one spot. Google says to "think of notebooks as personal knowledge bases shared across Google products, starting in Gemini." Gemini's Notebooks also sync with …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/tech/909031/google-gemini-notebooks-notebooklm)

Notebooks sound a lot like ChatGPT's Projects feature, which launched in 2024 and similarly lets users store things about a certain topic in one spot. Google says to "think of notebooks as personal knowledge bases shared across Google products, starting in Gemini." Gemini's Notebooks also sync with  …
Read the full story at The Verge.

creator: Boone Ashworth
title: (No AI) This AI Wearable From Ex-Apple Engineers Looks Like an iPod Shuffle
dc:creator: Boone Ashworth
content: Two former Apple Vision Pro developers made an AI wearable that only listens when you tap it. They hope to win where other AI gadgets have fumbled: privacy.
contentSnippet: Two former Apple Vision Pro developers made an AI wearable that only listens when you tap it. They hope to win where other AI gadgets have fumbled: privacy.
